us-politics.headline: US-Iran Tensions Spike as Deadline Looms and Jets Fall
us-politics.body: Trump warned Iran that 'time is running out' before 'all hell' rains down, while Senator Graham urged Tehran to 'choose wisely' ahead of a diplomatic deadline. The pressure campaign intensified after Iran downed US fighter jets, prompting former National Security Advisor John Bolton to suggest Trump may be in 'panic mode.' Secretary of State Rubio revoked the green cards of two relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani, who were then arrested by ICE - a move supporters frame as national security enforcement and critics see as political signaling. Representative Moulton argued Iran is currently winning the war of attrition. Analysts are divided on whether military pressure or diplomacy offers the cleaner path forward, with opinion pieces questioning what 'finishing the job' against Iran would even look like in practice.

sources.title: The US can beat Iran at its own game, and without boots on the ground
ICE arrests relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani living in US after Rubio revokes their green cards
Rubio revokes green cards of 2 Soleimani relatives amid Iran conflict
Graham backs Trump’s Iran ultimatum as deadline approaches: ‘Choose wisely’
In Iran, what does ‘finish the job’ even mean?
Trump warns Iran: ‘Time is running out’ before ‘all hell’ rains down
Moulton says ‘Iran is winning the war’ after US jets downed
Bolton: Trump likely in ‘panic mode’ after Iran attacks US fighter jets

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Cuts Domestic Spending, Boosts Defense and Prisons
us-politics.body: Trump unveiled a budget proposing a $1.5 trillion surge in defense spending paired with deep cuts to domestic programs. The proposal also includes a $152 million request to reopen Alcatraz as a high-security federal prison, a move the administration frames as part of its broader law enforcement push. Critics warn the domestic cuts will hit vulnerable populations hardest, while supporters argue the defense investment is overdue. Separately, a federal judge blocked a Trump administration effort that would have required colleges to certify they do not consider race in admissions - a ruling that preserves existing legal ambiguity on the issue. The week also saw the Supreme Court take up birthright citizenship, keeping constitutional questions about immigration at the center of national debate.

sources.title: Judge halts Trump effort requiring colleges to show they don't consider race in admissions
Trump unveils $1.5T defense surge, deep domestic cuts — what’s on the budget chopping block
Alcatraz could reopen as a 'state-of-the-art secure prison' under Trump's $152M budget request
Week in Politics: Trump's latest speech; SCOTUS on birthright citizenship; Pam Bondi

us-politics.headline: Global Standing Slips as China Overtakes US in Approval Ratings
us-politics.body: A new Gallup poll shows China has surpassed the United States in global approval ratings, a notable shift that reflects growing international unease with American foreign policy. The finding lands as the US faces simultaneous pressure points - escalating tensions with Iran, strained alliances, and a domestic political environment marked by sharp partisan conflict. Bipartisan concern also surfaced in the Senate, where Senators Wicker and Shaheen raised alarms about a planned US visit by Russian lawmakers, signaling that Russia policy remains a fault line cutting across party lines.

sources.title: China now tops US in global approval ratings: Gallup
Wicker, Shaheen raise concerns about US visit by Russian lawmakers

us-politics.headline: Republicans Eye Midterms Nervously as Democrats Maneuver
us-politics.body: Former White House press secretary Kayleigh McEnany warned publicly that the 2026 midterms 'will be hard' for Republicans, an unusually candid admission that reflects anxiety inside the party about voter sentiment. In Virginia, a new poll shows a narrow majority supports redistricting to favor Democrats, adding to GOP concerns about the electoral map. On the Democratic side, Representative Eric Swalwell's ongoing clash with the FBI is drawing scrutiny as he pursues a California governor bid, with opponents arguing the controversy undermines his candidacy. In Wyoming, the son of a Republican megadonor entered the race for an open at-large House seat, signaling that donor-class influence in GOP primaries remains strong heading into the next cycle.

sources.title: McEnany sounds alarm: Midterms ‘will be hard’ for Republicans
Swalwell-FBI clash draws attention to Trump foe’s California governor bid
Narrow majority in Virginia backs redistricting to favor Democrats: Poll
Son of Republican megadonor throws hat in the ring for open at-large House seat in Wyoming

au-politics.headline: Albanese Gambles on Gambling Ad Reform Before Election
au-politics.body:
Prime Minister Anthony Albanese faces a high-stakes decision on gambling advertising reform, with the Murphy Report's recommendations sitting on his desk ahead of the federal election. Labor has long promised action on the issue, but the party is caught between public health advocates pushing for a full ban and the commercial interests of broadcasters and sporting codes that depend on gambling revenue.

Supporters of reform argue the current saturation of gambling ads causes measurable harm, particularly to young Australians. Opponents warn that heavy-handed restrictions could damage free-to-air television and live sport. Albanese's handling of the report will be read as a signal of how much political risk he is willing to absorb before voters go to the polls.

sources.title: Trash or treasure? Albanese’s big gamble

au-politics.headline: Fuel Costs and University Decline Squeeze Young Australians
au-politics.body:
High fuel prices are cutting off some young Australians from work, with regional and outer-suburban workers hit hardest. For those without access to public transport, the cost of commuting is eroding take-home pay to the point where some are turning down shifts or leaving jobs entirely. The problem is sharpest in areas where driving is the only option.

Separately, Australia's university sector is under pressure from a different direction. A sharp drop in international student numbers, driven by tighter visa settings and reputational concerns, has exposed how financially dependent institutions became on overseas enrolments. Critics argue the system was hollowed out over years of underfunding. Universities now face difficult choices about course cuts, staff reductions, and their long-term academic standing.

sources.title: High fuel prices mean some young Australians cannot afford to get to work
The 'enshitification' of the system has left Australia's unis at a crossroad

au-politics.headline: Cocaine Boat's Secret Tunnels End in Sydney Arrest
au-politics.body:
A vessel described as having a "bizarre" journey from Panama has arrived in Sydney carrying what authorities say are tonnes of cocaine hidden in secret tunnels built into the hull. The MV Raider drew attention during its voyage before Australian Border Force and police moved to intercept it. The scale of the alleged haul makes it one of the larger maritime drug seizures in recent Australian history.

The case highlights the ongoing challenge of policing Australia's vast coastline against sophisticated smuggling operations. Investigators say the tunnel construction points to a well-resourced criminal network rather than an opportunistic operation. Charges are expected to follow as authorities work through the evidence.

sources.title: Secret tunnels and 'tonnes' of cocaine as boat's 'bizarre' journey ends in Sydney

world.headline: US and Israel Strike Iran as Pilot Search Continues
world.body:
The United States and Israel are conducting active military operations against Iran, hitting targets including a Tehran university, an Iranian bridge, and infrastructure near the Bushehr nuclear plant. A projectile struck close to Bushehr, killing one person, prompting the UN's nuclear watchdog to voice deep concern. A US fighter jet was also shot down over Iran, and a search operation described as harrowing is underway for the missing crew member.

Trump issued a 48-hour ultimatum to Tehran to reach a deal, while Secretary of State Marco Rubio moved to strip Soleimani's niece of US residency and authorities arrested other relatives of the late Iranian general. Iran responded defiantly, saying the US and Israel belong in the Stone Age. Italy's Meloni flew to Qatar to secure energy alternatives as the conflict rattles European supply chains.

sources.title: US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
Top diplomat Marco Rubio strips Qassem Soleimani’s niece of US residency
What we know so far about the US fighter jet shot down over Iran
New video reveals moment US-Israeli munitions impact Iranian bridge

UN watchdog voices 'deep concern' as Iran reports new attacks on nuclear plant
Iran says US, Israel belong in Stone Age after Tehran university strike

What we know so far about the search for missing US airman in Iran
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Italy’s Meloni meets Qatar emir to discuss energy issues amid Iran war

world.headline: Russia Kills at Least 15 in Ukraine as Zelenskyy Meets Erdogan
world.body:
Russian strikes across Ukraine killed at least 15 people in the past 24 hours, including five in an attack on a market. The strikes hit civilian areas, underscoring the continued targeting of non-military sites despite international pressure. Ukrainian President Zelenskyy met Turkish President Erdogan, likely seeking diplomatic leverage and continued military support.

The meeting signals Kyiv's ongoing effort to keep international allies engaged as the war grinds on.

sources.title: Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five
At least 15 killed in Russian attacks on Ukraine as Zelenskyy meets Erdogan

world.headline: Israel Expands Strikes Into Lebanon as Gaza Fighting Continues
world.body:
Israeli forces struck a building in Lebanon's Tyre and damaged a hospital there as a ground invasion advanced further into Lebanese territory. Separately, a drone strike in central Gaza engulfed a vehicle in flames. Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese remain displaced, with aid workers describing lives turned upside down.

The simultaneous fronts in Gaza and Lebanon reflect an expanding Israeli military posture with little sign of restraint. Three suspects were also remanded in UK custody following an attack on a Jewish charity, a case drawing attention to rising tensions beyond the immediate conflict zones.

sources.title: Vehicle engulfed in flames after Israeli drone strike in central Gaza
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Three suspects ordered to stay in UK custody over Jewish charity attack
Witness records Israeli strike on building in Lebanon’s Tyre
Israeli strikes damage hospital in Lebanon’s Tyre; ground invasion advances

world.headline: Alcatraz Revival, Mafia Arrest, and Other World Briefs
world.body:
President Trump requested $152 million to reopen Alcatraz as a functioning federal prison, reviving a proposal widely seen as symbolic but drawing serious budget scrutiny. In Italy, a fugitive mafia boss wanted for murder was arrested at a luxury villa on the Amalfi Coast after years on the run.

Senegal banned ministers from foreign travel as rising oil prices strain the government's finances. In Australia, officials urged citizens to continue Easter travel plans despite fuel shortages. US-South Africa relations remain strained over land reform disputes and diplomatic friction with the Trump administration.

sources.title: Australians told to continue Easter travel plans despite fuel shortages
Fugitive mafia boss wanted for murder arrested in Amalfi Coast luxury villa
Senegal bans ministers from foreign travel as oil price rise bites
Why have US-South Africa relations soured?
Trump seeks $152m to reopen notorious Alcatraz prison

ai.headline: Anthropic Squeezes OpenClaw Out of Claude Ecosystem
ai.body:
Anthropic is forcing Claude Code subscribers to pay extra for OpenClaw usage, a move critics are calling a de facto ban on the open-source agent protocol. The Verge and TechCrunch both covered the decision, which effectively walls off Claude from a growing ecosystem of third-party agents built around OpenClaw's open networking model.

The timing is notable. Anthropic is riding high in private markets, with investors circling despite competition from SpaceX's Starlink-adjacent ventures threatening to absorb capital. Locking Claude tighter to proprietary tooling while the company courts fresh funding signals a deliberate platform strategy. Meanwhile, hackers are distributing a leaked Claude Code build bundled with malware, adding a security headache to an already fraught week for the company.

sources.title: Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware

ai.headline: Claude Finds 23-Year Linux Bug; AI Code Quality Debate Sharpens
ai.body:
Claude Code surfaced a Linux vulnerability that sat undetected for 23 years, a striking demonstration of AI-assisted security research. The find comes alongside new academic work showing that simple self-distillation techniques meaningfully improve code generation in large language models, suggesting the ceiling on AI coding ability is still rising.

Not everyone is impressed with AI-generated output. A GitHub commit adding 12,000 AI-generated blog posts in one shot drew sharp criticism for flooding a repository with low-quality content. The contrast is stark: AI can quietly find bugs humans missed for decades, yet it also enables content spam at industrial scale. A detailed breakdown of coding agent architecture published this week helps explain both outcomes, tracing how agent design choices drive wildly different results.

sources.title: Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years
Simple self-distillation improves code generation
12k AI-generated blog posts added in a single commit
Components of a Coding Agent

ai.headline: AI Fakes and Copyright Trolls Are Targeting Independent Artists
ai.body:
A folk musician named Murphy Campbell found herself caught between two modern threats: AI-generated fakes of her music and a copyright troll exploiting the confusion. The Verge's reporting shows how small independent artists lack the legal resources to fight either problem, making them easy targets when the two collide.

The case lands as pressure grows to prove human authorship. The Verge separately reports on a nascent movement pushing for "human-made" labels on creative work, with designers and writers seeking ways to certify AI-free content. Demand for that kind of signal is rising fast, but no standard exists yet. The gap between the problem and any workable solution remains wide.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Nvidia eGPUs Come to Arm Macs; GPU Sharing Startup Launches
ai.body:
Apple has approved a driver enabling Nvidia external GPUs to work with Arm-based Macs, cracking open a hardware combination that was previously blocked. The move gives Mac users a path to serious local GPU power without waiting for Apple Silicon to close the gap on raw compute.

On the software side, a new service called sllm is letting developers split GPU nodes and share access with others, pitching unlimited tokens as the draw. A separate browser-based project brought Google's TurboQuant vector quantization to WebAssembly this week. Taken together, the flurry of GPU-related projects reflects how acute the compute access problem remains for developers working outside large cloud budgets.

sources.title: Apple approves driver that lets Nvidia eGPUs work with Arm Macs
Show HN: sllm – Split a GPU node with other developers, unlimited tokens
Show HN: TurboQuant-WASM – Google's vector quantization in the browser

btc.headline: Bitcoin Gains Political Backing as Safe-Haven Case Strengthens
btc.body:
Former UK Chancellor George Osborne has publicly backed Bitcoin as an alternative to what he calls failing financial systems, adding institutional credibility to a narrative that research from Mercado Bitcoin is now quantifying. The firm's analysis finds Bitcoin consistently outperforms gold and equities in the months following major global shocks - a pattern that cuts against its reputation as a pure risk asset.

The timing matters. Bitcoin shorts face $2.5 billion in potential liquidations if the price reaches $72,000, and five separate data sources flagged by CoinDesk suggest market liquidity is quietly thinning. Bears are exposed. A relatively small price move could trigger a cascade that accelerates the very rally skeptics are betting against.

sources.title: Bitcoin tends to outperform gold and stocks after global shocks, Mercado Bitcoin finds
Ex-UK Chancellor backs bitcoin as alternative to failing systems
Bitcoin shorts risk $2.5 billion liquidation at $72K: Are bears in danger?
Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside

btc.headline: Bitcoin's Big Traders Bleed $337M Daily Through Q1
btc.body:
Wealthy Bitcoin traders lost an average of $337 million per day in the first quarter of 2026, a figure that reframes the quarter's price action as something far more brutal than headline numbers suggested. Large holders absorbed the bulk of the damage while retail largely sat on the sidelines.

Meanwhile, pressure is mounting on corporate crypto treasuries to justify their existence. A CoinDesk opinion piece argues that simply holding digital assets is no longer enough - treasuries must now generate yield or strategic value to satisfy shareholders. Passive accumulation as a strategy is losing its defenders.

sources.title: Rich Bitcoin traders lost $337M daily in first quarter of 2026
Digital asset treasuries must now earn their keep

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Hit Legal Walls From Nevada to Asia
btc.body:
A Nevada judge extended a ban on Kalshi, rejecting the platform's argument that its event contracts fall outside gambling regulations. The ruling is a setback for the broader prediction market industry, which has spent years arguing its products are fundamentally different from sports betting. Courts are not convinced.

The legal pressure is not limited to the US. CoinTelegraph reports that prediction platforms are testing regulatory limits across strict Asian markets, where the line between financial instruments and gambling is drawn even more aggressively. Polymarket added to the industry's reputational headaches by pulling a market tied to a missing US pilot after public backlash - a reminder that legal exposure and ethical exposure often arrive together.

sources.title: Nevada judge extends ban on Kalshi, rejects event contract defense
Prediction markets are testing legal limits in strict Asian markets
Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash

btc.headline: Solana Faces Quantum Threat, Bitcoin Debates Node Philosophy
btc.body:
Solana's engineers are confronting an uncomfortable tradeoff: post-quantum cryptographic upgrades would significantly slow the network, undermining the speed that defines its competitive position. The dilemma is not hypothetical - quantum computing timelines are compressing, and blockchains that delay upgrades now may face harder choices later.

On the Bitcoin side, developer Jimmy Song is pushing for a dedicated conservative node client, one that resists protocol changes and prioritizes stability over new features. The argument is philosophical as much as technical. Song believes Bitcoin's value depends on predictability, and that existing clients are too permissive toward upgrades that could introduce unforeseen risk.

sources.title: Solana's quantum-threat readiness reveals harsh tradeoff: security vs speed
Jimmy Song explains why Bitcoin needs a 'conservative' node client

food.name: Mafaldine al Limone e Bottarga
food.description: Ruffled ribbon pasta tossed in brown butter, lemon zest, and shaved cured mullet roe for a briny, citrus-bright hit of umami
food.tip: Grate the bottarga over the finished dish off the heat — direct heat kills its delicate salinity and turns it bitter

exercise.name: The Turkish Get-Up Ladder
exercise.equipment: One kettlebell (or a filled water bottle as a substitute)
exercise.description: Perform Turkish get-ups starting at 1 rep per side, climbing to 5 and back down to 1, resting only as long as it takes to switch hands — a slow, full-body movement that builds shoulder stability, hip mobility, and floor-to-standing coordination simultaneously

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous systems of crayfish, exploited in medical devices for balance disorders, and studied in climate science to explain ice age cycles.
learn.why: It fundamentally challenges the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you understand it you start seeing its fingerprints everywhere from neuroscience to financial markets to how your own sensory system works
